votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It extracts the base-10 digits of n (which uniquely determines the result since trailing zeros do not affect the product or presence of odd digits) and checks if the result matches the expected product of odd digits.

The Python code correctly implements the little-endian digit extraction and the logic for calculating the product of odd digits. It properly handles the existence condition by constructing the canonical digit list and checks the result against the conditional logic defined in the Coq specification.

The Python code accurately implements the Coq specification. The helper functions match the Coq definitions, and digits_spec correctly extracts the base-10 digits of n to compute the expected result, which is invariant to trailing zeros in the digit list.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. For any positive integer n, the Python digits_spec constructs the unique decimal digit list ds (least significant digit first) such that val10(ds) = n and digits_range(ds) holds. It then computes the expected result as prod_odd(ds) if has_oddb(ds) else 0, matching the Coq spec. The helper functions val10, digit, digits_range, prod_odd, and has_oddb all produce the same concrete results as their Coq counterparts. For n <= 0, Python returns False, which is correct since no ds can satisfy decimal_digits (which requires n > 0).

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. For any positive integer n, the Python digits_spec constructs the unique decimal digit list ds (least significant digit first) such that val10(ds) = n and digits_range(ds) holds. It then computes the expected result as prod_odd(ds) if has_oddb(ds) else 0, matching the Coq spec. The helper functions val10, digit, digits_range, prod_odd, and has_oddb all produce the same concrete results as their Coq counterparts. The key insight is that for any positive n, there exists exactly one valid ds satisfying decimal_digits(n, ds), and Python constructs exactly that ds.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. For any positive integer n, the Python digits_spec constructs the unique decimal digit list ds (least significant digit first) such that val10(ds) = n and digits_range(ds) holds. It then computes the expected result as prod_odd(ds) if has_oddb(ds) else 0, which matches the Coq spec. The helper functions val10, digit, digits_range, prod_odd, and has_oddb all produce the same concrete results as their Coq counterparts. For n <= 0, Python returns False, which is correct since no ds can satisfy decimal_digits (which requires n > 0).

Both define digits least-significant-first, require n>0, and compute res as the product of odd decimal digits of n (0 if none). Although the Coq spec quantifies over any valid digit list, the result is invariant under trailing zeros, so the Python’s canonical-digit choice yields the same judgment for all inputs.

Python mirrors Coq’s little-endian digit encoding, digit range check, odd test, product, and has-odd logic. digits_spec constructs the canonical decimal digits for n>0 and compares res to the product of odd digits (or 0 if none), which is invariant under the Coq spec’s existential choice of digits (trailing zeros don’t affect the result). Thus results match for all inputs.

Both use little-endian base-10 digits, same digit range, odd-check and product logic. The Coq spec’s existential over digit lists only varies by trailing zeros, which don’t affect has_oddb or prod_odd, so Python’s canonical choice yields the same result for all inputs.
